title: Declarative animation system, easing, camera, and transitions
short_description: Ship the deterministic animation vocabulary — enter/exit families (fade, slide, pop), transforms along paths, camera pan/zoom/focus, scene transitions (cut, crossfade, wipe, paper-slide) — as pure functions of time with a standard easing library, choreographed entirely from beat structure.
long_description_md:
# Declarative animation system, easing, camera, and transitions

## Why this task exists

Phase 1 needs more than a static scene graph: it needs a small, agent-legible vocabulary for turning beat-annotated narration into motion. This task supplies that vocabulary without introducing imperative frame callbacks, wall-clock behavior, or style-specific rendering logic. An agent should be able to declare that a node fades in during a beat, slides or pops into place, follows a path, and exits later; the compiler and renderer should then have one deterministic answer for every property at every time.

That is essential to anima-ai's verification-first architecture. The timeline compiler lowers narration beats into property tracks, the renderer evaluates those tracks to paint frames, and Phase 2 verification inspects the same resolved state for timing closure, visibility violations, pacing, overlap, and continuity. If animation behavior lives in ad hoc callbacks or renderer-only branches, there is no inspectable contract for an agent or verifier to reason about.

The task also establishes the first generic camera and scene-transition semantics. A multi-scene explainer needs intentional framing and a deterministic handoff between scenes; otherwise Phase 1 can produce isolated static frames but not the 60–90 second coherent video required by the roadmap.

## Roadmap fit

This is a direct Phase 1 capability-3 deliverable. The phase's completion fixture explicitly requires shapes, text, groups, entrances/exits/transforms, camera pan/zoom, and scene transitions rendered into a 1080p30 MP4. It also supports the Phase 1 timing requirement that all choreography derive from a beat-annotated script rather than manually calculated frame numbers.

The task consumes `timeline-compiler`'s resolved beat spans, compiled-track representation, and timing-invariant checks. It adds stable animation declarations and deterministic lowering/evaluation semantics through the compiler's documented action extension point; it must not duplicate WPM estimation, hold/pause handling, scene-duration arithmetic, or the compiler's ownership of conflict reporting.

It complements the typed scene graph: animations target stable node IDs and typed transform properties, while the later geometry resolver turns resolved transforms into output-pixel bounds for both rendering and verification. The rasterizer and PyAV encoder consume the resulting sampled state but are separate Phase 1 work.

## Authoring and timing contract

Animation declarations are immutable, typed, JSON-serializable values. They are attached to beat actions and expressed in beat-local timing terms that the timeline compiler resolves into seconds; authored APIs must not accept raw frame numbers or require global timeline timestamps. A declaration may not silently escape its owning beat or scene boundary. Invalid target IDs, unsupported target properties, invalid durations, and actions that conflict with the compiler's exactly-one-resolved-value invariant must fail deterministically with the affected beat and node/property identified.

Lowering produces inspectable per-property tracks or equivalent immutable evaluated state with stable ordering. At any resolved time `t`, evaluating an animation, camera, or transition is a pure function of its serialized declaration, compiled timing, and explicit input state. It must not read the clock, process environment, mutable global state, ambient randomness, dictionary iteration order, or renderer state. Boundary behavior is explicit: before an animation its start state is returned, after it its end state is returned, and values at exact keyframe boundaries are stable.

A declaration names an easing by stable registry ID, never by an arbitrary Python callable. Ship a documented standard library including `linear`; `quad`, `cubic`, `quart`, `quint`, `sine`, `expo`, and `circ` in `in`, `out`, and `in_out` variants; plus `back`, `elastic`, and `bounce` variants for deliberate overshoot or bounce. The registry normalizes progress deterministically, returns finite values for valid input, has exact endpoint behavior, and distinguishes monotonic easings from intentional overshooting easings. Unknown easing IDs are typed authoring errors rather than fallbacks.

## Animation vocabulary

Ship the following generic, style-agnostic declarations and their lowering semantics:

- Enter/exit families: `fade` animates opacity between transparent and the node's resolved base opacity; `slide` animates from or toward an explicit direction-and-distance offset while preserving the node's intended transform; `pop` animates scale between a documented positive collapsed scale and the node's intended scale, with its opacity behavior explicit in the declaration contract. Exit variants are deterministic inverses rather than independent renderer effects.
- Transform animations: move, scale, and rotate target only their declared transform components and compose predictably with the node's base transform and group-local transform model. Move-along-path supports a deliberately typed deterministic motion-path subset—at minimum ordered line segments and cubic Bézier segments—with exact endpoints and deterministic parameterization. Path sampling must not depend on Skia, platform geometry behavior, or floating-point collection ordering.
- Camera animations: model a generic camera state with center and strictly positive zoom. Support pan, zoom, and focus-on-node. Focus declarations reference a stable node ID plus explicit padding/fit policy and resolve target bounds through a narrow geometry-resolver protocol, so camera code neither reimplements text/path bounds nor creates a renderer dependency. The resolver receives world-space state before camera application, avoiding a camera/focus feedback loop.
- Scene transitions: model transition state separately from node tracks and lower transitions from beat-derived scene-boundary timing. `cut` changes scenes without interpolation; `crossfade` exposes deterministic outgoing/incoming opacity; `wipe` exposes a directional progress/clipping contract; and `paper_slide` exposes directional incoming/outgoing panel offsets. The rasterizer later consumes these states; no transition may be hidden in backend-specific compositor code.

The public inspection surface must expose the chosen easing IDs, resolved timing, target IDs/properties, camera state, and transition state, so `anima inspect --compiled` and Phase 2 rules can explain choreography without rendering pixels.

## Determinism and verification consequences

The compiled result must be byte-stable for identical project source, seed, engine version, and timeline inputs. Although these Phase 1 core animations use no randomness, they must remain compatible with the project-level seeded-RNG contract and may not introduce a second randomness mechanism. Equivalent repeated evaluations at sampled boundaries and interior points must return equal values.

The implementation must preserve the data-model timing invariants that every animated property resolves to exactly one value at every time and no animation targets a node outside its visibility window. It should provide the resolved inputs Phase 2 needs for structural rules, but it does not implement those verification rules itself.

## Explicitly out of scope

- Wobbly stroke behavior, paint reveal, paint textures, glyph tracing, and other procedural paint rendering; these belong to Phase 3.
- Map-specific fill sweeps, border morphs, arrow growth, map viewport flights, and cross-scene map-state persistence; these belong to the map engine in Phase 4.
- Style-pack transition defaults and pack-specific animation sets; core provides neutral semantics, while packs provide choices later.
- Text/path bounding-box calculation, camera-to-pixel projection, Skia rasterization, static-layer caching, contact-sheet generation, video encoding, and atomic output writes.
- Timeline WPM estimation, TTS alignment, narration parsing, raw frame-number APIs, a GUI timeline, or audio rendering.
- Phase 2 structural, layout, pacing, and continuity rule implementation beyond preserving the compiled state they will inspect.
roadmap_phase: Phase 1: Scene graph, timeline, and first pixels

deliverables.description: A typed, immutable standard easing registry with stable serializable IDs for linear; quad/cubic/quart/quint/sine/expo/circ in/out/in_out variants; and back/elastic/bounce variants. Easing evaluation must be a finite deterministic pure function with exact documented endpoints, normalized input behavior, explicit overshoot semantics, and typed rejection of unknown IDs or invalid inputs.

Declarative beat-attached node-animation specifications and timeline-compiler lowering for fade, slide, and pop enters/exits plus move, scale, rotate, and move-along-path transforms. Use stable node/property targets, beat-derived resolved spans, typed line/cubic-Bézier motion paths with exact endpoints, canonical ordering, and deterministic sampled track evaluation that preserves the compiler's exactly-one-value and visibility-window invariants.

A generic camera-state contract and animation evaluator supporting pan, positive zoom, and focus-on-node through a narrow world-bounds resolver protocol, plus beat-boundary scene-transition declarations/evaluators for cut, crossfade, directional wipe, and directional paper-slide. The resulting camera and transition states must be inspectable renderer-neutral data, with no Skia-specific composition logic.

A deterministic multi-beat, multi-scene compiler fixture that exercises every shipped easing family category, node animation family, transform path, camera verb, and transition type; samples beat boundaries and interior times; proves repeated compilation/evaluation produces identical canonical results; and confirms no state is sourced from a clock, raw frame counter, or ambient randomness. Keep strict typing and linting green for the public animation surface.

verification.command: python -m pytest -q tests/integration/test_animation_timeline.py tests/animation/test_determinism.py && ruff check src/anima/animation tests/animation tests/integration/test_animation_timeline.py && mypy --strict src/anima/animation
